name: Tiered sensing architecture for longitudinal parasomnia capture
consequences: Direct effect: expands data richness across streams and improves the chance of capturing infrequent events. Downstream implication: raises staffing needs and data handling complexity, while elevating privacy-safeguard requirements during cross-stream synchronization.
options: Standardize nightly low-burden monitoring using dry-electrode EEG headbands, contact-free mattress sensors, and infrared cameras, with automated quality checks to flag when data meet preset completeness thresholds.
Prioritize enhanced nights for PSG only when sufficient event density is detected or technician flags, avoiding routine full PSG unless clinically warranted.
Integrate escalation nights triggered by algorithmic event cues to deploy full clinical-grade PSG and re-train scorers on contentious events.
review: The tiered sensing approach improves data richness while raising operational complexity and data management overhead; the three options leave gaps in real-time privacy controls and cross-stream synchronization during escalation events.

name: Data governance and privacy posture for residential parasomnia data
consequences: Direct effect: strengthens compliance and enables controlled data sharing, reducing privacy risk. Downstream implications: slower data release cycles and increased administrative load that competes with recruitment and analysis funding; potential restrictions on downstream collaborations if governance delays occur.
options: Implement a rigorous de-identification pipeline and access-controlled data sharing framework to enable de-identified physiological data release while maintaining participant privacy.
Limit access to video streams and sensitive metadata to a vetted researcher cohort through formal data-use agreements and institutional review board approvals.
Adopt a data governance charter with quarterly privacy audits and clear escalation paths for data breach risks, balancing openness with participant protection.
review: The data governance options enable safer data sharing but risk slowing external validation; the three approaches do not specify how to handle data-use revocation or emergency access during crises.

name: Staged capacity expansion aligned with data quality and staffing signals
consequences: Direct effect: expands recruitment capacity and data volume, enhancing statistical power for longitudinal phenotyping. Downstream implication: increases operational complexity and higher staffing requirements; the trade-off risks diluting focus on data quality if expansion outpaces gating metrics.
options: Adopt a sequential expansion plan that only scales to 12 suites after achieving stable data quality metrics, acceptable false-alarm rates, and safe staffing ratios during the initial pilot.
Proceed with immediate expansion to 12 suites but implement phased occupancy limits and rigorous scheduling controls to preserve safety margins and data integrity.
Maintain eight on-site suites and pursue external study partnerships or satellite sites to boost enrollment without increasing on-site capacity.
review: Expansion strategy increases capacity but may outpace data quality controls; the options do not specify how to re-balance staffing and scheduling during rapid scaling.

name: Semi-automated event-triage tool development pipeline
consequences: Direct effect: reduces manual review time and accelerates processing, but introduces risk of missed subtle events if thresholds are miscalibrated. Downstream implications: requires ongoing labeled data, validation, and monitoring to maintain accuracy; potential model drift can affect generalizability.
options: Build a rule-driven event triage framework that flags high-likelihood parasomnia episodes for immediate review by the night team.
Train supervised models on labeled PSG and sensor data to rank probable events, with human scoring serving as final arbiter for ambiguous cases.
Establish a continuous calibration loop by re-annotating a subset of events and updating the triage model as new data accumulate.
review: The triage tooling reduces manual load but introduces risk of missed events if thresholds are too aggressive; the options do not specify how to monitor drift or ensure cross-population generalization.

name: Pre-admission pathway optimization for NREM parasomnia cohort
consequences: Direct effect: improves enrollment efficiency and improves the representativeness of the recruited cohort. Downstream implications: increases screening burden on clinical staff and potential delays if consent and eligibility processes become bottlenecks; risk of inadvertent selection bias if criteria tighten.
options: Standardize structured clinical interview and collateral history protocols with centralized screening to accelerate eligibility determination.
Engage regional neurologists and sleep clinics with formal referral agreements and shared scheduling to broaden recruitment reach.
Offer flexible compensation and participation logistics to increase enrollment among underrepresented groups while maintaining ethical standards.
review: Pre-admission optimization improves enrollment efficiency but may increase screening burden and selection bias if processes become too rigid.

name: Governance and risk management framework for high-stakes residential study
consequences: Direct effect: enhances oversight, accountability, and proactive risk mitigation. Downstream implications: could slow pivots and decision-making during growth phases if board processes are not streamlined; may constrain rapid response to emerging findings.
options: Institute defined decision gates with board-reviewed milestones for budget reallocations and recruitment thresholds.
Implement quarterly risk heatmaps and contingency plans covering safety, data privacy, and regulatory compliance.
Establish a rapid escalation protocol for safety incidents, with predefined authorities for temporary halt and mitigations.
review: Governance enhances risk mitigation yet may slow strategic pivots; the options do not specify emergency decision rights or thresholds for rapid board action.

name: Dynamic sensing density for residential parasomnia capture
consequences: Pulling this lever increases data richness by adding enhanced nights and optional full PSG, improving event capture. A downstream implication is higher staffing demands and storage costs, which pressures the budget and throughput. It may also elevate participant burden if escalations disrupt routine comfort, potentially reducing enrollment velocity.
options: Maintain baseline low-burden monitoring for all participants and reserve enhanced nights only when automated alerts indicate probable events, minimizing risk of participant fatigue.
Schedule a fixed cadence of enhanced nights at pre-defined weeks to balance data completeness with participant comfort and operational feasibility.
Implement on-demand escalation nights triggered by automated event likelihood scores to target data-rich windows while preserving overall burden.
review: Trade-off: increasing data richness raises participant burden and operating costs, while the three options lack a clear method to calibrate alert thresholds and minimize false positives without sacrificing yield.

name: Adaptive staffing ratio linked to real-time event load
consequences: Adapting staffing to event load improves safety and data throughput but increases wage variability and scheduling complexity. Downstream, overtime costs and staffing burnout become possible trade-offs if demand spikes persist. This lever also influences recruitment and retention strategies.
options: Staff nights using cross-trained technologists with flexible shifts to cover peak event windows and provide backup coverage.
Maintain constant night staffing with on-call reserve to handle surges in adjudication workload and avoid coverage gaps.
Implement predictive staffing using historical event rates to pre-assign technicians and reduce idle time and overtime expenses.
review: Trade-off: flexible staffing improves responsiveness but pushes wage variability and scheduling complexity; the three options omit explicit overtime controls and burnout mitigation, risking longer shifts and staff dissatisfaction.

name: Data privacy-by-design augmentation for bedside video controls and data rights
consequences: Strengthening privacy controls protects participant rights and can increase participant trust, yet may reduce data utility or sharing flexibility. Downstream, stricter controls could slow external collaborations and complicate data governance. Operational clarity on consent for reuse is also affected.
options: Institute tiered consent with bed-partner involvement and clearly delineated data-use scopes, including replay rights.
Implement automated local redaction of video metadata with strict access controls and audit trails.
Adopt a decentralized data-access model where de-identified data remains within the Bonn campus network and external sharing requires approvals.
review: Trade-off: privacy controls can reduce data utility; the three options do not specify how consent granularity aligns with future research reuse or cross-institution collaboration.

name: Cross-site benchmarking collaboration with external sleep centers
consequences: Engaging external centers improves standardization and benchmarking, but raises coordination overhead and data-sharing complexity. A downstream implication is potential delays from legal reviews and harmonization cycles. It also elevates governance overhead.
options: Form a formal data-use consortium with adjacent German sleep centers to standardize scoring and share de-identified datasets.
Arrange quarterly cross-site audits of scoring reliability to benchmark inter-rater agreement and harmonize criteria.
Pilot remote harmonization workshops with external scorers to accelerate consensus building.
review: Trade-off: standardization improves comparability but increases coordination overhead; the three options leave unaddressed data-sharing restrictions and legal risk across sites.

name: Pre-admission screening acceleration pathway
consequences: Accelerating pre-admission processing reduces time to enrollment and aligns screening with study cadence, but risks compromising safety filtering if criteria become too lenient. Downstream, faster entry may require tighter in-study monitoring and enhanced risk controls. It also impacts patient flow and staffing planning.
options: Streamline specialist clinical interview and collateral history with standardized questionnaires to shorten pre-admission processing.
Introduce telemedicine pre-screenings to reduce in-person visits while maintaining data quality.
Implement risk-based triage to fast-track high-probability candidates and slower-path for uncertain cases.
review: Trade-off: faster pre-screening may introduce selection bias; the three options neglect how to preserve safety exclusions while maintaining enrollment velocity.

name: Resource-lean expansion plan gated by data quality metrics
consequences: Expanding capacity only after meeting data quality thresholds preserves scientific integrity and budget discipline, but slows scaling and time-to-impact. Downstream, prolonged gating can threaten funding continuity and stakeholder confidence if milestones drift. It also shapes future expansion economics.
options: Expand physical capacity only after achieving predefined annotation throughput and low false alarm rates across two consecutive cohorts.
Maintain existing capacity while testing virtual data augmentation to validate methods without new rooms.
Schedule phasing of renovation tied to pilot milestones through a governance-approved plan.
review: Trade-off: gating expansion by quality metrics slows scaling and increases time to impact; the three options do not specify funding resilience or contingency plans beyond pilot-phase milestones.

name: Tiered PSG escalation cadence tied to event-flag triggers
consequences: Direct effect: full polysomnography deployment is reserved for nights with high signal density, improving data resolution for flagged events. Downstream implication: increases scheduling complexity and overnight equipment utilization, potentially delaying recruitment throughput unless event yield is consistently high. Trade-off: prioritizes data quality over throughput, risking under-sampling of participants with infrequent events.
options: Deploy full polysomnography on nights when integrated sensor signals exceed predefined confidence thresholds, ensuring high-resolution data for flagged events.
Maintain a fixed cadence where PSG is performed on fixed nights regardless of signals to ensure comparability across participants.
Implement a rolling two-night validation window to determine PSG deployment, balancing escalation with baseline data collection.
review: Primary trade-off is data depth versus throughput, and the options leave unaddressed whether escalation criteria could introduce selection bias or how to maintain representativeness for low-signal participants.

name: Privacy-preserving data sharing policy with tiered access
consequences: Direct effect: enforces privacy controls by limiting exposure of video and raw signals. Downstream implication: external collaboration may shrink or slow, but reduces risk of data misuse and participant re-identification. Trade-off: balancing openness with privacy could constrain external validation opportunities.
options: Share only de-identified physiological streams with external repositories under data-use agreements, while withholding raw video in all public datasets.
Publish synthetic-augmented summaries for external dissemination, reserving full dataset access for approved researchers under strict governance.
Create a controlled-access data enclave with tiered user permissions and audit trails to balance openness and participant privacy.
review: Trade-off centers on openness versus privacy; the options omit details about data-use governance, reproducibility guarantees, and how external researchers obtain timely access.

name: Inter-rater reliability calibration program with structured audits
consequences: Direct effect: increases scoring consistency across raters and nights, improving phenotyping reliability. Downstream implication: imposes extra scoring and adjudication time, potentially delaying data release and increasing labor cost. Trade-off: calibration cadence may not keep pace with protocol amendments.
options: Institute quarterly double-scoring rounds where a random subset of nights is scored by dual raters with blinded reconciliation to raise agreement.
Adopt a consensus-first approach where senior scorers resolve discrepancies after independent initial scoring to accelerate finalization.
Integrate automated quality checks that flag scoring disagreements and route to adjudication within 24 hours for rapid resolution.
review: Primary trade-off is efficiency versus alignment across scorers; options lack a plan for training turnover or handling drifts when new criteria are introduced.

name: Regional clinician referral network expansion
consequences: Direct effect: broadens recruitment channels and accelerates enrollment; downstream: coordination overhead and potential heterogeneity in pre-screening processes. Trade-off: requires standardized procedures to avoid eligibility drift.
options: Establish formal referral agreements with regional neurologists and sleep clinics, including standardized pre-screening checklists and consent templates.
Pilot a community outreach program targeting patient groups with embedded screening pathways to capture broader potential participants.
Implement a centralized regional intake hub with multilingual staff to streamline eligibility assessment and scheduling.
review: Primary trade-off is recruitment breadth versus screening consistency; the options do not specify governance for cross-site data collection or quality control across partners.

name: Sensor fusion standardization for data integrity
consequences: Direct effect: enforces consistent data formats, timestamps, and labeling; downstream: reduces interoperability risk but slows onboarding of new sensors and adaptation to tech upgrades.
options: Adopt EEG-BIDS-compatible pipelines with validated timestamp synchronization across dry-electrode, bed-sensor, video, and environmental streams.
Allow phased integration of new sensors under a formal pilot validation before full deployment to minimize data gaps.
Create a formal sensor integration playbook with versioning, compatibility checks, and regression tests for all hardware changes.
review: Primary risk is misalignment during sensor upgrades; the options do not specify rollback plans or cross-compatibility testing across legacy data.

name: Safety and staffing risk buffer reconfiguration
consequences: Direct effect: increases on-site safety margins through flexible staffing and contingency plans; downstream: elevates fixed costs and reduces scheduling flexibility during low-demand periods.
options: Establish a dynamic on-site nurse and technician shift model that expands coverage during high-event weeks to maintain safety margins.
Retain an on-call roster and cross-train staff to cover multiple roles during disruptions or equipment downtime.
Implement automated alarm escalation, safety drills, and incident reporting to sustain safety without excessive headcount.
review: Trade-off is cost containment versus readiness; options do not specify a maximum staffing threshold or risk-based triggers for adjusting headcount.
